paper_title: DynMuon: A Dynamic Spectral Shaping View of Muon

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: In recent years, Muon has emerged as the dominant method for training large language models, and transformers more broadly. The essential difference, when compared to standard gradient descent methods, is to replace the usual update matrix $M=UΣV^\top$ with its polar factor $UV^\top$. In this work, we consider a class of Muon-like updates, where we replace the update $M$ with $UΣ^p V^\top$ for some parameter $p$. We call this a "spectral-shaping" operation, and develop a theory of how to pick $p$ which depends on (a) local curvature of the loss function, (b) noise stemming from stochastic gradients and label noise, and (c) training stage. Our theory and experimentation reveal a previously overlooked behavior: positive $p$ helps early by emphasizing high-curvature directions and accelerating signal contraction, while mildly negative $p$ helps later by reallocating update strength toward low-curvature directions that still contain useful training signals. Building on the insight, we propose DynMuon, an efficient dynamic spectral shaping method that schedules $p$ from positive to mildly negative over training. Extensive experiments across model sizes, architectures, and training settings show that DynMuon consistently achieves lower validation loss than Muon, while requiring 10.6-26.5% fewer steps to reach the same target loss.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: DynMuon: Dynamic Spectral Shaping View of Muon Fangzhou Wu1, Rikhav Shah2,, Sandeep Silwal1,, Qiuyi (Richard) Zhang3, 1University of WisconsinMadison, 2MIT, 3Elorian AI fwu89@wisc.edu, rdshah@mit.edu, silwal@cs.wisc.edu, richard@elorian.ai

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: In recent years, Muon has emerged as the dominant method for training large language models, and transformers more broadly. The essential difference, when compared to standard gradient descent methods, is to replace the usual update matrix = ΣV with its polar factor . In this work, we consider class of Muon-like updates, where we replace the update with ΣpV for some parameter p. We call this spectral-shaping operation, and develop theory of how to pick which depends on (a) local curvature of the loss function, (b) noise stemming from stochastic gradients and label noise, and (c) training stage. Our theory and experimentation reveal previously overlooked behavior: positive helps early by emphasizing high-curvature directions and accelerating signal contraction, while mildly negative helps later by reallocating update strength toward low-curvature directions that still contain useful training signals. Building on the insight, we propose DynMuon, an efficient dynamic spectral shaping method that schedules from positive to mildly negative over training. Extensive experiments across model sizes, architectures, and training settings show that DynMuon consistently achieves lower validation loss than Muon, while requiring 10.626.5% fewer steps to reach the same target loss.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Muon has recently shown strong empirical performance for LLM training [11]. At high level, for each matrix-valued model parameter, Muon forms momentum-averaged gradient matrix = ΣV and replaces it with its polar factor . The resulting update preserves the singular directions while flattening the singular values, and has been shown to improve convergence and training stability across model scales [17, 1]. This flattening of singular values naturally suggests studying more general spectral transformations, which we call spectral shaping, for matrix-valued optimization, and exploring how the relative weighting of spectral directions affects training dynamics. Recent work has explored spectral shaping in limited capacity by studying fixed power-law singularvalue shapings [28, 3], or transformations that suppress dominant spectral subspaces [9, 37]. However, they still treat spectral shaping as static, seeking better fixed spectral transformations. Thus, it is not clear whether single shaping rule is desirable as training dynamics evolve [28]. Relatedly, prior work also lacks training-dynamics model of spectral shaping, and therefore does not characterize how the relative influence of different spectral directions evolves across training [9, 37]. This leaves central question unresolved, which is the main focus of our paper: How should Muon-style spectral shaping adapt across training stages, if at all? 6 2 0 2 6 1 ] . [ 1 9 0 1 7 1 . 5 0 6 2 : r Our Results To answer this question, we generalize Muon as one point in power-law family of what we call spectral-shaping operations, where is the spectral exponent: ), D(p) := ΣpV , Σp = diag(σp 1, . . . , σp (1) The remaining authors are listed alphabetically. Preprint. for matrix-valued update = ΣV . This is demonstratively very expressive family of operations: = 1 gives an inverse-spectrum update, = 0 recovers Muon, and = 1 corresponds to the standard SGD-style updates. To understand training dynamics as function of p, we develop simple noise-aware local modelling that interprets spectral shaping as curvature-dependent reweighting of the update along local curvature directions of the loss landscape. Along these directions, it jointly tracks the residual signal, i.e., the remaining parameter distance to nearby local optimum, and the stochastic gradient noise introduced by minibatch sampling. This mode-wise decomposition reveals an interesting signalnoise trade-off: decreasing increases residual-signal contraction in flat, lower-curvature directions, but also amplifies noise along these same directions. It further suggests that as training progresses, the remaining residual signal becomes less concentrated in high-curvature directions and relatively more prominent in small, flat curvature directions (Section 2.1). This leads to stage-dependent finding: positive helps early by emphasizing high-curvature directions and accelerating residual-signal contraction, whereas mildly negative helps later by refocusing the updates towards flat directions that still carry useful residual signal. Thus, we uncover previously overlooked late-stage training behavior: dynamically shifting emphasis toward flat directions further improves optimization, stage-dependent advantage that fixed transformations such as Muon cannot capture. Our empirical observations support the predictions of our modelling and show that adapting across training improves performance (Sections 2.2 to 2.4). Motivated by these observations, we propose DynMuon, dynamic spectral shaping algorithm that adapts the spectral exponent over training (Section 3). It leverages simple decreasing logistic schedule for p, interpolating from positive values early in training to mildly negative values later. To realize our scheduled spectral shaping efficiently, DynMuon extends NewtonSchulz approximation to approximate ΣpV for varying values of p, avoiding full SVD and retaining the per-step cost as Muon. Extensive experiments across model sizes and architectures show that DynMuon consistently achieves lower validation loss than Muon across training settings, while reaching target losses with up to 26.5% fewer training steps. To summarize, our main contributions are: 1. We introduce dynamic spectral-shaping perspective for matrix-valued updates, reframing Muonstyle optimization from fixed spectral operation into the adaptive problem of choosing suitable spectral exponent as training dynamics evolve. 2. We develop noise-aware local model (section 2) that explains how the right choice for the spectral exponent changes across training stages through trade-off between residual-signal reduction and stochastic-noise amplification across local curvature directions. 3. Guided by this model, we uncover surprising stage-dependent regime: positive spectral exponents help early training, whereas (previously overlooked) negative exponents improve late-stage optimization by emphasizing flat directions that retain useful residual signal. 4. In Section 3, we propose DynMuon, simple and efficient dynamic spectral-shaping algorithm that adapts the spectral exponent throughout training, yielding consistent improvements over Muon across training settings (Section 4). 1.1 Other Related Work Muon and Spectral Shaping. Recent works have shown that orthonormalizing matrix-shaped momentum can substantially improve neural network / LLM training [11, 17]. Muon uses Newton Schulz iterations to produce orthonormalized updates for hidden layers, and recent works explain its effectiveness through trust-region, norm-constrained, and spectral preconditioning perspectives [13, 27, 3, 22]. growing line of work studies variants of matrix updates, including specific fixed positive choices of within the ΣpV , as well as spike-aware, anisotropic, or mode-guided transformations [28, 9, 37, 20]. More broadly, matrix-aware optimizers such as Shampoo, SOAP, and PolarGrad exploit non-diagonal geometry and spectral structure beyond coordinate-wise scaling [8, 30, 35, 15]. These methods establish spectral structure as an important design axis, but they largely use fixed or task-specific transformations. In contrast, this paper studies whether the preferred spectral bias should evolve across training stages, systematically developing dynamic spectral shaping rule. Dynamic Scheduling in Optimization. Dynamic schedules are widely used to adapt optimization behavior across training phases, including learning-rate annealing, restarts, warmup, cyclical policies, iteration-dependent scaling, and studies of stage-dependent subspace behavior [18, 31, 32, 21, 38, 6, 4]. Unlike schedules that mainly modulate scalar hyperparameters, DynMuon dynamically schedules 2 the spectral shape of matrix-valued updates, allowing the emphasis we put over different spectral directions to evolve across training stages. See extended related work in Section G.

sections.title: 2 Motivating Dynamic Spectral Shaping with an Idealized Model
sections.content: Although Muon-style methods [11, 17] have shown strong empirical gains from the orthonormalized update, recent analyses suggest that their effectiveness can vary across training conditions [28, 5]. This raises the question of whether fixed Muon-style spectral shaping remains optimal throughout training. Motivated by this, we systematically vary spectral component in the spectral-shaping family ΣpV and study its effect on optimization. Before proposing our method, we first develop simple noise-aware local model, using standard local quadratic and gradient-noise approximation, to isolate how controls the trade-off between useful training signal and gradient noise (Section 2.1). We note that our modelling and analysis make several simplifying approximations (which we empirically validate). Our goal is not to provide full end-to-end convergence theory, but rather to introduce minimal set of reasonable approximations that yield mechanistic insights into the role of the spectral exponent in training dynamics. In particular, our modelling predicts two stage-dependent regimes: positive can benefit early training, whereas mildly negative can improve late-stage training. We empirically validate these predictions in Sections 2.2 to 2.4, showing that our simplified modelling can serve as useful and predictive guide for designing dynamic spectral shaping. 2.1 Noise-Aware Local Model for Spectral Shaping Our starting point is to consider one such weight matrix Wt Rmn at training step t, and take its stochastic gradient Gt = UtΣtV as the update matrix. Applying spectral shaping with exponent gives D(p) := UtΣp t . With the learning rate η, the parameter update is 2 Gt. Wt+1 = Wt ηD(p) = Wt η(GtG ) p1 (2) Let L(W ) denote the population loss, and let be nearby local minimizer with L(W ) 0. We define the residual signal as Et := Wt , measuring the remaining error relative to . To analyze how the shaped update in Equation (2) affects optimization, we study the evolution of this residual signal under the update. Since the update is driven by the gradient, we relate the gradient to the residual signal Et by performing locally linear approximation of the population gradient around [25]. We further use one-sided Kronecker-factored approximation to the local Hessian action, in the spirit of K-FAC [24]. We apply this approximation over short training window in which the effective local curvature is empirically stable (see Figure 8 for empirical support): L(Wt) L(W ) + 2L(W )[Et] κtHEt, (3) where = QΛQ is normalized effective local curvature matrix with Λ = diag(h1, . . . , hm), hi (0, 1], and maxi hi = 1. The scalar κt > 0 captures the overall curvature scale. The eigenvectors of define the modes of the local loss landscape, where each mode corresponds to one curvature direction and hi measures the curvature along that direction. Modes with larger hi are called strong modes and modes with smaller hi are called flat modes. To account for stochasticity in the actual training update, we decompose the stochastic gradient Gt into the population gradient and zero-mean noise Ξt, following standard unbiased SGD assumptions [2]. Plugging into (3) gives: Gt = L(Wt) + Ξt κtHEt + Ξt. (4) Since the shaped update depends on (GtG ) to the effective local curvature. Motivated by the common use of gradient second moments as Fisher-type p1 proxies for local curvature [14, 23], we use curvature-aligned surrogate: (GtG ) 2 , where αt > 0 is scalar factor. This approximation models spectral shaping as curvaturedependent reweighting. The underlying gradient-curvature alignment is empirically supported in 2 , we relate the spectral structure of GtG p1 2 α p1 2 Appendix Figure 8. Substituting this approximation into Equation (2), absorbing α effective learning rate ηt, and using Equation (4), the residual signal evolves as Et+1 = Et ηtH p1 2 (κtHEt + Ξt) = ηtκtH p+1 Et ηtH p1 2 Ξt. (5) (cid:16) (cid:17) p1 2 into the Mode-Wise SignalNoise Tradeoff. Based on the intuitive idealized analysis above, we now arrive at the main training dynamics equation that we focus on. Since acts through powers of the curvature 3 matrix = QΛQ in Equation (5), its effect can vary across curvature directions. This motivates mode-wise analysis, where we project the residual signal Et and noise Ξt onto the eigenbasis of H. Define (cid:101)Et := QEt and Zt := QΞt. Let δi,t and ξi,t denote the i-th coordinates of (cid:101)Et and Zt. Then mode evolves as δi,t+1 = (cid:16) 1 ηtκth p+1 2 (cid:17) δi,t ηth p1 ξi,t. (6) We next consider the squared residual for each mode. Assume that the mode-wise noise is conditionally zero-mean with E[ξi,t δi,t] = 0 and E[ξ2 i,t δi,t] = ci,t, where ci,t denotes the noise level of mode at step t. Then Equation (6) gives E[δ2 i,t+1 δi,t] = (cid:16) 1 ηtκth p+1 2 (cid:17)2 i,t + η2 δ2 hp1 ci,t. (7) Thus, induces mode-wise signalnoise trade-off. The deterministic multiplier 1ηtκth(p+1)/2 coni trols residual-signal contraction: within the stable range (0, 1), larger h(p+1)/2 makes this multiplier smaller and contracts δi,t faster. Increasing therefore favors contraction in strong, high-curvature modes, whereas decreasing increases the relative contraction strength in flat modes. However, the stochastic term is scaled by hp1 , so decreasing also amplifies noise most strongly in flat modes. Without noise, = 1 would maximize contraction in flat modes; with noise, the choice of must balance residual-signal contraction against noise amplification. This decomposition also provides the basis for the mode-wise predictions tested in Section 2.3, where we empirically estimate the residual-signal energy δ2 i,t and noise level ci,t during training. Takeaway: The spectral exponent controls mode-wise signalnoise tradeoff. Larger accelerates residual signal contraction in strong modes, while smaller shifts more emphasis toward flat modes but also amplifies noise along them. 2.2 Why Slightly Negative Spectral Exponent Is Preferred in the Late Stage Building on the mode-wise signalnoise tradeoff in Equation (7), we assess how different spectral exponents affect training performance. We focus on [1, 1], covering the representative cases in Section 1.1. At high level, training improves when the update reduces residual signal in modes where substantial signal remains. Since varying changes which modes are emphasized, the preferred choice of should depend on how the residual signal is distributed across modes during training. p+1 2 Why Residual Signal Concentrates in Flat Modes Late in Training. For hi (0, 1], the contraction strength increases with hi. Thus, the residual signal in strong modes tends to decay earlier, whereas the signal in flat modes decays more slowly and can remain substantial later in training. This suggests that the residual signal becomes relatively more concentrated in flat modes as training progresses. This is also consistent with the local gradient approximation in Equation (3). Projecting the population gradient onto mode gives gi,t κthiδi,t, hence δi,t gi,t . Thus, for comparable κthi projected gradient magnitudes, smaller curvature hi corresponds to larger residual signal. When Mildly Negative Exponent Helps in the Late Stage. Once the residual signal becomes more concentrated in flat modes in the late training stage, it can be beneficial to place relatively more emphasis on those modes rather than on strong modes whose residual signal has already decayed. Decreasing achieves this effect by allocating relatively more contraction to flat modes. However, from Equation (7), lowering also increases the noise level, especially in flat modes. Thus, negative can improve over = 0 only when the residual signal in flat modes is large enough relative to the noise. This also explains why the exponent should be only mildly negative: otherwise, noise amplification can outweigh the benefit from reducing the residual signal and degrade optimization. 2.3 Validating Predictions of Our Model We empirically examine whether the signal-noise conditions predicted by our model arise under Muon (p = 0). Using GPT-style model with hidden dimension 768, we freeze all parameters except one target matrix in the final transformer block and estimate its mode-wise curvature, residual signal, and noise. Full setup and estimation details are provided in Section A. 4 Figure 1: Validation of the mode-wise model predictions. Left: The residual-shift metric Πt decreases during training and becomes negative, indicating that the residual signal shifts from strong, high-curvature modes toward flat modes. Middle: The noise-adjusted signal in flat modes remains substantially positive, indicating that flat modes retain the residual signal that is large relative to their noise level. Right: The flat-mode signal-noise advantage remains positive, showing that flat modes have favorable signal-noise tradeoff relative to strong modes in the late stage. Figure 2: Training performance of stage-dependent spectral shaping. Left/Middle: Mildly negative exponents improve late-stage validation loss in both last-layer and full-model training, while overly negative and late-positive exponents degrade performance. Right: Early positive improves fullmodel training, with the positive-to-negative schedule achieving the lowest validation loss. In contrast, simply doubling the early learning rate or reversing the schedule from negative to positive performs worse than Muon. The panels use capped y-axes; uncapped versions are shown in Appendix Figure 11. Empirical Modes and Proxies. In our analysis, modes are curvature directions, which are expensive to track directly in practice. We therefore use the singular directions of the Muon update as empirical modes, since spectral shaping reweights singular values along these directions. For each empirical mode at step t, we estimate local curvature ˆhi,t, mode-wise noise level ˆci,t, and residual-signal energy ˆδ2 i,t. Specifically, ˆhi,t is estimated via Hessian-vector products, ˆci,t from the variance of independent mini-batch gradient projections, and ˆδ2 i,t from fixed-probe gradient projections using the local gradient approximation in Equation (3). Validating Residual-Signal Concentration in Flat Modes. To test whether the residual signal concentrates in flat modes, we sort modes by curvature ˆhi,t and define and as the top-8 highest-curvature and bottom-8 lowest-curvature modes, respectively. We summarize each bucket by the median log residual signal energy and define the residual shift as Πt = mediS log ˆδ2 i,t. Thus, Πt < 0 indicates that flat modes have larger residual signal than strong modes. Figure 1 (left) shows that Πt steadily decreases and becomes mostly negative after roughly 500 steps, supporting the predicted late-stage concentration of residual signal in flat modes. i,t mediF log ˆδ2 Validating Flat-Mode Signal After Accounting for Noise. Since negative exponent also amplifies noise, we measure whether the flat-mode residual signal remains useful after accounting for noise. Therefore, we define the mode-wise noise-adjusted signal ui,t := log ˆδ2 i,t log ˆci,t, which measures the residual signal relative to the noise, and the flat-mode signal-noise advantage Ωt = mediF ui,t mediS ui,t, which compares this noise-adjusted signal between flat and strong modes. Figures 1 (middle, right) show that the noise-adjusted signal ui,t in flat modes remains substantially positive and that Ωt quickly increases and remains positive. This indicates that flat modes retain the residual signal large enough relative to noise, matching the condition under which mildly negative exponent can improve over the Muon choice = 0. We provide additional empirical analysis in Section B. Training Performance for Mildly Negative p. We next test whether the mode-wise analysis translates into improved training performance by switching from = 0 to negative exponent at 5 step 500, when the residual signal begins to concentrate in flat modes. We first compare negative exponents {0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1} in both last-layer-only and full-model training. As shown in Figure 2 (left, middle), mildly negative provides the best performance: = 0.1 and = 0.25 outperform the Muon default = 0, while more aggressive choices such as = 0.75 and = 1 become unstable and perform much worse. This agrees with our analysis: moderate emphasis on flat modes helps late-stage optimization, but overly negative amplifies noise and degrades performance. Conversely, switching to positive exponent (p = 0.25) at the same step degrades full-model training performance, as shown in Figure 2 (middle). This result further supports our prediction that late-stage gains specifically stem from emphasizing flat modes. Takeaway: In late training, residual signal shifts toward flat modes, making slightly negative spectral exponent preferable because it allocates more contraction to these directions. However, this preference is mild: if is too negative, noise amplification can outweigh the contraction benefit and hurt training. This matches our empirical results, where mildly negative values improve performance while more aggressive negative values degrade it. 2.4 Why Positive Spectral Exponent Can Help in the Early Stage Beyond the late-stage preference for mildly negative exponent, we ask whether the Muon default = 0 is also optimal early in training. Our analysis suggests otherwise: early residual signal can remain concentrated in high-curvature modes, and increasing accelerates contraction of these strong-mode residual signal while reducing the noise-amplification factor . Thus, before the residual distribution shifts toward flat modes, positive exponent can accelerate early optimization by prioritizing the strong-mode residual signal with limited noise amplification. p1 2 Training Performance with an Early Positive Exponent. To test this prediction, we use simple two-stage schedule that sets = 1 for the first 500 steps, then switches to either = 0 or = 0.1. As Figure 2 (right) shows, both early-positive schedules achieve lower validation loss than the fixed Muon baseline, supporting the view that prioritizing strong modes early improves the optimization trajectory. Notably, the schedule transitioning from = 1 to = 0.1 yields the best performance. This validates our stage-dependent picture: positive exponent accelerates early training, while mildly negative exponent improves late-stage optimization as the residual signal becomes relatively more concentrated in flat modes. We further test two alternative explanations. First, to rule out the possibility that the early positive merely acts as larger effective learning rate, we test Muon variant that doubles the learning rate during the first 500 steps while keeping = 0. This variant performs worse than fixed Muon, indicating that the gain from an early positive is distinct from simple step-size scaling. Second, we evaluate reverse schedule that switches from = 0.1 to = 1 at step 500. This schedule also performs worse than fixed Muon, consistent with our analysis that positive should be applied early, while mildly negative is beneficial only later. Takeaway: positive early-stage exponent helps reduce strong-mode residual signal before the remaining residual signal shifts toward flat modes, yielding lower validation loss than fixed Muon.

sections.title: 3 DynMuon: Dynamic Spectral Shaping
sections.content: The above analysis and observations motivate DynMuon (Algorithm 1 in Section C), which dynamically adapts spectral shaping by monotonically decreasing the spectral exponent from positive early-stage value to mildly negative late-stage value, while maintaining computational efficiency. Logistic Scheduling of the Spectral Exponent. Although the residual-signal distribution across modes can guide the choice of p, estimating it online would require additional forward and backward passes. We therefore use simple logistic schedule to approximate smooth decreasing transition of pt over training without this extra cost. Given the current training step and total steps , we set ut = t/T τ , at = 1 1 + exp(ut) , pt = pmin + at(pmax pmin). Here, τ controls the transition point and controls the transition width, with smaller producing sharper switch. We set pmax = 1 and pmin = 0.25, where pmin = 0.25 is the best-performing negative exponent based on our observations in Section 2.2 (we also ablate pmin; see Section 4). 6 Figure 3: Validation loss trajectories across three model scales trained on 10B tokens. DynMuon consistently achieves the lowest validation loss across all three model scales. Efficient Updates. Given scheduled exponent pt, exact SVD can realize the corresponding fractional spectral shaping, but it is computationally expensive. Muon avoids SVD by approximating the = 0 polar update with small fixed number of NewtonSchulz (NS) iterations, but it does not directly support arbitrary exponents. To obtain an efficient implementation, DynMuon uses an equivalent factorization of the target spectral shaping (Appendix Algorithm 2). For the normalized input Xn = X/XF and := XnX ) 1 2 Xn = ΣpV = (XnX ) 2 (XnX (8) Since Yµ = 1 2 Xn corresponds to the Muon update, it can be efficiently approximated by NS. It remains to approximate the left correction 2 . When this factor acts as mild spectral correction, it introduces only small adjustment on top of the Muon update. We thus approximate it by second-order Taylor expansion around the identity I. Letting = I, δ = p/2, we have CYµ. For Rmn with n, DynMuon adds only one polynomial correction of cost O(m2n + m3) on top of NS computations. Thus, our method has the same asymptotic complexity as NS-based Muon. Figure 5 (right) shows that our approximation closely tracks exact SVD throughout training. 2 δ(δ 1)E2. The target spectral shaping is then given by (cid:101)X = Xp 2 = + δE + 1 2 Xn. 2 1 , we first write 2 Xn = (XnX ) p1 Stable Anchoring for Positive Exponents. DynMuon implements the spectral shaping scheduled exponent pt through simple stage-wise scheme. The main reason is stability: when pt is positive and sufficiently large, the correction factor 2 is no longer mild adjustment to the Muon update, so the Taylor approximation around can become unreliable. To avoid this instability, DynMuon anchors the positive regime to two stable operators (lines 710 in Algorithm 1). DynMuon uses the original update when pt 0.25. For pt [0, 0.25), DynMuon applies standard NS orthogonalization, recovering the Muon-style update. For pt [pmin, 0), the exponent is only mildly negative, so DynMuon uses the efficient spectral approximation described above for continuous schedule.

sections.title: 4 Evaluation
sections.content: Models and Datasets. We evaluate DynMuon on two decoder-only Transformer families: GPTstyle models at multiple scales following modded-nanoGPT [10] and Qwen-style model, with detailed configurations summarized in Tables 2 and 3. The GPT-style models use rotary position embeddings [34], RMSNorm, and squared ReLU MLPs [33]. The Qwen-style model uses prenormalized Transformer blocks with RMSNorm, grouped-query attention, and gated SiLU MLPs. All models use sequence length 1024 and global batch size 512. Our main experiments use 10B tokens from FineWeb, and we additionally evaluate on FineWeb-Edu [26]. To study training-budget scaling, we vary the number of training tokens from 2.5B to 20B. Baselines. We compare DynMuon against Muon [11] and AdamW [19]. Muon is our primary and most directly relevant baseline, while AdamW serves as standard, widely used optimizer baseline. We further include NorMuon [16] as an additional Muon-variant baseline in Section E. Unless otherwise specified, the default learning rates are 0.01 for Muon and DynMuon, and 0.002 for AdamW. We also vary learning rates for all methods. By default, DynMuon uses pmax = 1 and pmin = 0.25. Additional evaluation setup details are provided in Section D. Main Results. We train GPT-style models at three scales on FineWeb using both 10B and 20B token budgets. As shown in Figure 3, DynMuon consistently achieves the lowest validation loss across all three model scales compared with baselines. The improvement over Muon is clear in the late training stage, where scheduling the spectral exponent toward mildly negative values provides 7 Table 1: Performance and efficiency of DynMuon relative to Muon across GPT-style model scales. Steps to Target uses the validation loss reached by Muon at 80% of training as the target. Step Saving reports the relative step reduction, and Per-Step Time is the average ms/step. Tokens Method (Size) Best Val. Loss () Steps to Target () Step Saving () Per-Step Time (ms) 10B 20B Muon (127M) DynMuon (127M) Muon (601M) DynMuon (601M) Muon (1.1B) DynMuon (1.1B) Muon (127M) DynMuon (127M) Muon (601M) DynMuon (601M) Muon (1.1B) DynMuon (1.1B) 3.190 3.171 2.872 2.858 2.788 2. 3.139 3.124 2.802 2.797 2.722 2.713 16000 12500 16000 13950 16000 30400 22350 30400 25000 30400 26450 0.0% 21.9% 0.0% 12.8% 0.0% 10.6% 0.0% 26.5% 0.0% 17.8% 0.0% 13.0% 1142.4 1150.3 4121.7 4200.1 6883.3 7055. 1137.3 1151.8 4126.2 4184.8 6889.77 6910.1 Figure 4: DynMuon outperforms Muon over architectures, training-token budgets, and learning rates. stable advantage. Table 1 quantifies the practical significance of these gains in terms of both step efficiency and runtime overhead. For each model size and token budget, we define fixed target as the validation loss reached by Muon at 80% of training, and record the first step at which DynMuon reaches it. Across model scales, DynMuon reaches the target 10.626.5% earlier than Muon, requiring substantially fewer training steps to reach the same validation loss. Meanwhile, DynMuon has per-step time ratio of only 1.0031.025 relative to Muon, indicating negligible additional runtime cost. Thus, DynMuon improves final performance and step efficiency with minimal per-step overhead. Model Architecture. To evaluate transferability beyond GPT-style models, we train 171M Qwenstyle decoder-only Transformer using the configuration in Table 3. As shown in Figure 4 (left), DynMuon consistently achieves lower validation loss than Muon, suggesting that dynamic spectral shaping transfers across decoder-only architectures. Training Token Scale. To evaluate robustness across training budgets, we vary the number of training tokens from 2.5B to 20B for the 601M model. As shown in Figure 4 (middle), DynMuon consistently achieves lower validation loss than Muon across all tested budgets, suggesting that the benefit of dynamic spectral shaping is not tied to particular training horizon. Learning Rate. We test learning-rate robustness on the 127M model under the 10B-token budget by sweeping Muon and DynMuon over learning rates from 0.003 to 0.04. As shown in Figure 4 (right), DynMuon outperforms Muon across all tested learning rates and has flatter curve near its optimum, indicating lower sensitivity to learning-rate choice. Training Dataset. We test corpus robustness by replacing FineWeb with FineWeb-Edu on the 127M model while keeping all other settings unchanged. As shown in Figure 5 (left), DynMuon consistently outperforms Muon on FineWeb-Edu, with the advantage becoming more pronounced in the late stage. This suggests that the benefit of dynamic spectral shaping is robust to changes in the training corpus. Ablation on pmin. We vary the scheduling endpoint pmin on the 127M model. As shown in Figure 5 (middle), mildly negative choices outperform Muon, with pmin = 0.25 achieving the best validation loss. More aggressive negative choices, e.g., pmin = 0.5, perform worse, consistent with our analysis that overly negative exponents can degrade training performance. Comparison with Exact Spectral Operations. We compare DynMuon with an exact-SVD implementation of the same dynamic spectral schedule. As shown in Figure 5 (right), DynMuon closely Figure 5: Additional experiments for DynMuon across corpora, pmin choices, and spectral-shaping implementations. Left: DynMuon outperforms Muon on FineWeb-Edu. Middle: mildly negative pmin values perform best. Right: our spectral shaping approximations closely tracks exact SVD. Figure 6: Ablation of spectral scheduling strategies and logistic schedule parameters (τ, w). matches exact SVD in validation loss, and both outperform Muon. Since exact SVD is roughly 3 slower, DynMuon captures the benefit of dynamic spectral shaping at much lower cost. Ablation on Spectral Scheduling, Logistic Parameters (τ, w). We ablate both the scheduling strategy and the logistic schedule parameters (τ, w). As shown in Figure 6 (left), our default logistic schedule outperforms standard Muon and two ablations: an abrupt switch from = 1 to = 0.25 at step 500, and fixed negative schedule with = 0.25 throughout training. The abrupt schedule underperforms the logistic schedule, suggesting that smooth transition between spectral shaping is more effective than sharp switch. The fixed negative schedule performs substantially worse, showing that negative shaping throughout training is insufficient and supporting our stage-dependent design. Figures 6 (middle, right) further show that DynMuon is reasonably robust to the transition point τ and transition width w, with the best performance observed around τ = 0.04 and = 0.04. Additional Results Additional results in Section show that DynMuon consistently outperforms Muon across random seeds with low variance, and also improves over NorMuon. Robustness Across Loss Objectives. In Section F, we evaluate on larger family of the CEBrier loss interpolations [7], to test whether mild late-stage negative shaping remains beneficial beyond standard Cross-Entropy (CE). Interestingly, we find that across these objectives, mildly negative exponents also consistently improve over the corresponding = 0 baseline, while overly negative exponents degrade performance, suggesting that this benefit extends across probability-space losses.

sections.title: 5 Conclusions and Future Directions
sections.content: We studied broader power-law spectral shaping family, ΣpV . Our noise-aware local model reveals stage-dependent signal-noise trade-off: early training benefits from positive that emphasizes high-curvature directions, while late training benefits from mildly negative that reallocates update strength towards flat directions. We proposed DynMuon, which efficiently schedules from positive to mildly negative values during training. Experiments across model scales, architectures, and training settings show that DynMuon consistently achieves lower validation loss than Muon. Our experiments focus on validating the hypothesis that practical improvements are obtainable by varying over training. We show that this schedule outperforms fixed choices and the standard = 0 Muon update, but it is not the final word on the matter. Indeed, the best choice of may depend on training-time dynamics that cannot be fully anticipated in advance. This suggests interesting directions for future work: selecting online based on observed optimization statistics. Such methods could use more expensive signals, provided they are applied only occasionally rather than at every training step. Since the useful range of appears relatively small, we conjecture that future near-optimal adaptive Muon-style scheduler may only need to re-estimate occasionally during training.

Validation Details in Section 2 A.1 Validation Setup To validate the predictions of our model, we run experiments on GPT-style model with hidden dimension 768, 12 layers, and 6 attention heads [1], trained on FineWeb [26]. We consider two settings: last-layer-only training and full-model training. For last-layer-only training, we freeze all model parameters and optimize only one selected matrix-valued parameter in the final Transformer block. We train for 6000 steps in the last-layer-only setting and 10000 steps in the full-model setting, using up to 3B training tokens. For optimization, we use learning rate of 0.01 for matrix-valued parameter updates under both Muon and stage-wise spectral shaping. For non-matrix parameter groups, we use AdamW with learning rate of 0.001. We apply weight decay of 0.01 to the main matrix parameter groups. The learning rate uses linear warmup for the first 1% of training steps, followed by cosine decay with final warmdown ratio of 0.2. For these validation experiments, we use exact SVD to compute spectral shaping for different exponents. For the experiments in Section 2.2, we switch from = 0 to negative exponent at step 500 and compare {0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1} in both last-layer-only and full-model training. For the validations in Section 2.4, we use simple two-stage schedule that sets = 1 for the first 500 steps and then switches to either = 0 or = 0.1. A.2 Empirical Modes and Proxies In our analysis, modes are curvature directions, which are expensive to track directly in practice. We therefore use the singular directions of the Muon update as empirical modes, since spectral shaping reweights singular values along these directions. For efficiency, at each step t, we retain the top-k directions with = 256 and represent each as Bi,t := ui,tv i,t. For each Bi,t, we estimate the empirical local curvature using Hessian-vector product: This gives the curvature scale along the empirical mode Bi,t. ˆhi,t = 2L(Wt)Bi,t, Bi,t, We estimate the mode-wise noise level by measuring how much the gradient projection varies across independent mini-batches. Specifically, for nb = 32 mini-batches, we compute and define the noise level proxy as the sample variance i,t := G(b) g(b) , Bi,t, ˆci,t := Varb[nb][g(b) i,t ]. To estimate residual signal energy, we use separate fixed probe set of 8 mini-batches. Let gprobe denote the gradient projection on this fixed probe set. Since the probe gradient is averaged over fixed batch set, we use it as low-variance proxy for the population-gradient projection along Bi,t. Using the local relation gi,t κthiδi,t, we estimate ˆδ2 i,t := (gprobe Here, ˆhi,t is the HVP-based empirical curvature and already includes the local curvature scale. )2/ˆh2 i,t, i,t i,t 13 Figure 7: AdamW learning-rate sweep on the 127M GPT-style model, with the best validation loss achieved at 2 103. Figure 8: Empirical support for curvature stability and gradient-curvature alignment. Left: The median of log ˆhi,t over the retained empirical modes changes only mildly within the 4k6k step window, supporting our approximation that the effective local curvature can be treated as approximately fixed over short windows. Middle: Pearson () and Spearman () correlations across retained empirical modes between the local curvature proxy log ˆhi,t and the gradient second-moment proxy log ˆmi,t, where ˆmi,t := ˆci,t + (gprobe )2, remain strongly positive throughout training. Right: At representative step (t = 5900), the scatter plot of log ˆmi,t versus log ˆhi,t shows clear positive relationship. The left panel supports the local-stability assumption for the effective curvature. The middle and right panels support the gradient-curvature alignment used in the curvature-based approximation to the spectral preconditioner in Equation (5). i,t Additional Analysis in Section Stability of Effective Directional Curvatures. To examine the local-stability assumption in Equation (3), we track mediani[k] log ˆhi,t over the 4k6k step window, where ˆhi,t denotes the empirical local curvature estimated along the corresponding direction Bi,t, as defined in Section 2.3. As shown in Figure 8 (left), this median changes only mildly over this window, supporting our use of an approximately fixed effective local curvature over short training windows. Gradient Second Moments Align with Curvature. To support the curvature-alignment approximation in Section 2.1, we compare ˆmi,t with ˆhi,t over the retained empirical modes. For each retained empirical mode i, define ˆmi,t := ˆci,t + (gprobe )2, i,t where ˆci,t estimates the mode-wise noise level and gprobe estimates the population-gradient projection. Thus, ˆmi,t estimates the gradient second moment along that empirical mode. At each saved step, we compute Pearson and Spearman correlations over the retained empirical modes between log ˆmi,t and log ˆhi,t. As shown in Figure 8 (middle), these correlations remain strongly positive throughout training, providing empirical support for the approximation that gradient second moments are aligned with the local effective curvature. Figure 8 (right) further illustrates this alignment at representative i,t 14 Figure 9: Trends in the estimated noise exponent βt and the noise-curvature fit R2 during training. The power-law relationship between noise and curvature remains stable and pronounced throughout training. Figure 10: Impact of batch size on the preferred spectral exponent p. Left: best validation loss as function of under different batch sizes. Right: the optimal selected by the best validation loss for each batch size. Smaller batch sizes induce higher gradient noise and favor mildly negative values of closer to 0, consistent with our analysis that noise amplification limits the benefit of overly negative spectral exponents. step (t = 5900), showing strong positive relationship between log ˆmi,t and log ˆhi,t over the retained empirical modes. Noise-Curvature Scaling. Motivated by prior work on the geometry of gradient noise, we examine ˆhβt how noise level varies with curvature across modes by fitting power-law relation ˆci,t Nt i,t. The exponent βt describes the curvature dependence of noise, where positive βt means that the raw noise level is larger on high-curvature modes and smaller on flat modes. Figure 9 (left) shows that βt remains stable around 1.4, while Figure 9 (right) shows consistently high R2 values, indicating that this power-law relation provides reliable description of the mode-wise noise structure. Thus, although decreasing amplifies noise more strongly on flat modes, their raw noise level remains comparatively smaller, leaving room for slightly negative spectral shaping to exploit the flat-mode residual signal. Impact of Gradient-Noise Amplification on the Preferred Spectral Exponent p. To investigate how gradient-noise amplification affects the preferred spectral exponent in the late stage, we vary the batch size from 21 to 27 to induce different noise levels. We compare negative spectral exponents {0.1, 0.25, 0.5} with Muon (p = 0) in last-layer training. As shown in Figure 10, smaller batch sizes, which induce higher gradient noise, favor mildly negative exponents closer to 0: the best exponent is = 0.1 when the batch size is 2, and shifts to = 0.25 when the batch size is 15 Figure 11: Training performance of stage-dependent spectral shaping. Left/Middle: Mildly negative exponents improve late-stage validation loss in both last-layer and full-model training, while overly negative and late-positive exponents degrade performance. Right: Early positive improves fullmodel training, with the positive-to-negative schedule achieving the lowest validation loss. In contrast, simply doubling the early learning rate or reversing the schedule from negative to positive performs worse than Muon. Algorithm 1 DynMuon Algorithm 2 FastSpectral 1: Input: Update matrix , step t, total steps , schedule parameters (pmax, pmin, τ, w) 2: /* Logistic Scheduling */ 3: (t/T τ ) /w 4: 1/(1 + exp(u)) 5: pt pmin + a(pmax pmin) 6: /* Positive Anchoring */ 7: if pt 1/4 then return 8: 9: else if pt 0 then 10: 11: else 12: return FastSpectral(M, pt) return NewtonSchulz(M ) 1: Input: Target matrix X, spectral exponent 2: Xn X/XF , 3: /* Compute Muon Update */ 4: Yµ NewtonSchulz(Xn) 5: /* Low-Order Correction */ 6: XnX 7: 8: δ p/2 9: + δE + 1 10: /* Rescaling */ 11: (cid:101)X Xp 12: return (cid:101)X 2 δ(δ 1)E2 CYµ 16. As the batch size further increases to 128, the preferred exponent becomes more negative, with = 0.5 achieving the best validation loss. This trend is consistent with our analysis: negative spectral shaping can improve late-stage optimization by emphasizing flat modes, but overly negative exponents also amplify noise and can degrade performance, especially when the gradient-noise level is high. These results also suggest that reducing gradient noise, for example, through larger batch sizes, may make more negative spectral exponents beneficial.

sections.title: C DynMuon Algorithm Details
sections.content: We provide the full and efficient implementations of DynMuon in Algorithms 1 and 2. To facilitate reproducibility, we release lightweight implementation of these core algorithmic components, including the spectral-exponent schedule and the fast spectral-shaping approximation. The implementation is modular and plug-and-play: it only requires matrix-valued update as input, applies the scheduled spectral-shaping transform, and returns the shaped update for use in existing training code.

sections.title: D Experimental Details
sections.content: Models. We consider two decoder-only Transformer families: GPT-style architecture following the modded-nanoGPT setup [1], and Qwen-style architecture. The GPT-style models use GPT2 tokenizer with vocabulary size of 50304, rotary position embeddings [34], non-parametric RMSNorm, bias-free linear layers, and squared ReLU activations in the MLP blocks [33]. The 16 Figure 12: Mean validation loss across three random seeds. Shaded regions indicate one standard deviation, showing that DynMuon consistently outperforms Muon with very low seed variability. Figure 13: Comparison with NorMuon on the 127M model. DynMuon outperforms both Muon and NorMuon in validation loss. Qwen-style models use pre-normalized residual Transformer blocks with grouped-query attention, rotary position embeddings, and gated MLP with SiLU activation, followed by final RMSNorm layer and bias-free output head. As shown in Tables 2 and 3, we evaluate three GPT-style model scales and one Qwen-style model scale. The GPT-style models use dmodel = 512, 1280, and 1792, while the Qwen-style model uses dmodel = 512. Across all models, we use the same training setup, with global batch size of 512, per-device batch size 64, and sequence length 1024. Baselines. We compare DynMuon against two baselines: Muon [11] and AdamW [19]. For Muon and DynMuon, we use default learning rate of 0.01, and for AdamW, we use default learning rate of 0.002. We use weight decay of 0.01 for the main matrix parameter groups. Since Muon and DynMuon are designed for matrix-valued parameter updates, we use AdamW as the default scalar optimizer with learning rate of 0.001 for parameters outside the main matrix groups, including the embedding table and output head. We tune the learning rate over {0.003, 0.005, 0.01, 0.02, 0.04} for Muon and DynMuon, and over {104, 3 104, 5 104, 103, 2 103, 4 103, 8 103} for AdamW. As shown in Figure 7, AdamW achieves its best validation loss at learning rate 2 103. We use this tuned AdamW baseline when comparing against Muon and DynMuon in the main experiments. For DynMuon, we use = 0.01, τ = 0.02, pmax = 1, and pmin = 0.25 by default. For all methods, we use linear warmup for the first 0.01 of training steps, followed by cosine decay over the remaining steps, with final warmdown ratio of 0.2. Dataset. We train our models on pretraining datasets FineWeb and FineWeb-Edu [26]. Unless otherwise specified, our main setting uses training budget of 10B tokens of the FineWeb dataset. To study the effect of data scale, we vary the training budget from 2.5B to 20B tokens, corresponding to 5K, 10K, 20K, and 38K training steps, respectively. Devices. We use NVIDIA H200 GPUs for all experiments. 17 Figure 14: Robustness of mild negative spectral shaping across loss objectives. We plot the best validation CE relative to the corresponding = 0 baseline for each λ. Negative values indicate improvement over Muon. Mildly negative exponents remain beneficial across the CEBrier interpolation, whereas overly negative exponents degrade performance.

sections.title: E Additional Experiments
sections.content: Seed Robustness. To assess the robustness of DynMuon to training randomness, we run Muon and DynMuon with three different seeds {0, 1, 42}. Figure 12 reports the mean validation loss with one-standard-deviation bands across seeds. DynMuon consistently outperforms Muon, and the very small across-seed variance suggests that the improvement is robust to training randomness rather than seed-specific effects. Comparison with NorMuon. We further compare DynMuon with NorMuon [16], recent Muon variant that augments Muon orthogonalization with neuron-wise normalization based on secondmoment statistics. We implement NorMuon following its original algorithmic design in our controlled setting. As shown in Figure 13, DynMuon consistently achieves lower validation loss than both Muon and NorMuon.

sections.title: F Discussion on Robustness Across Loss Objectives
sections.content: The preferred spectral exponent may depend not only on the optimizer but also on the loss objective, since different losses induce different gradient and noise structures. Cross-Entropy (CE) emphasizes poorly predicted target tokens through the log ˆyc term, whereas squared-error-like probability losses impose smoother penalties on probability errors. These differences can alter the distribution of the residual signal and stochastic noise across spectral directions. Since controls the relative update strength across these directions, we investigate whether the mild negative regime identified above is specific to CE or remains beneficial under other probability-space objectives. To investigate this question, we use simple parameterized family of probability-space losses that interpolates between CE and squared-error-like objective: Lλ(y, ˆy) = (1 λ)CE(y, ˆy) + λBrier(y, ˆy), (9) where ˆy denotes the predicted probability distribution and Brier(y, ˆy) = (cid:80) i(yi ˆyi)2 is the squared error on predicted probabilities [7]. The endpoint λ = 0 recovers the standard CE objective, while λ = 1 gives the Brier score, probability-space analogue of MSE. By varying λ, we systematically change how the loss weights prediction errors and test whether the preferred mild-negative spectral regime remains robust. We use the same last-block target-matrix setting as in Section 2.2, but train for 3000 steps. For each loss interpolation parameter λ {0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0}, we sweep late-stage exponents {0, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0}. As before, we use = 0 before the switch step and apply the target exponent afterward. Because the training objective changes with λ, objective values are not directly comparable across different loss choices. We therefore evaluate all runs using validation CE as common metric, since it is the standard language-modeling measure of next-token predictive quality and is directly tied to perplexity [12]. For each configuration (λ, p), we report the difference between its best validation CE and that of the corresponding = 0 baseline under the same λ. Results in Figure 14 show that mildly negative exponents consistently improve upon the Muon baseline across the CEBrier interpolation, while more aggressive negative exponents such as = 0.75 and = 1 degrade performance. This suggests that the late-stage benefit of mild negative spectral shaping is not specific to standard cross-entropy, but persists across this family of probability-space loss objectives. Parallel Training. DynMuon preserves the matrix-wise update structure of Muon and only changes the local spectral shaping rule through the scheduled exponent pt. It therefore introduces no additional cross-layer or cross-device coupling beyond Muon, and should be compatible with existing Muonstyle parallel training implementations.

sections.title: G Extended Related Work
sections.content: Orthonormalization-Based Optimizers for LLM Training. Recent work has shown that explicitly orthonormalizing matrix-shaped momentum can substantially improve optimization in large-scale neural network training [11, 17, 36, 29]. In particular, Muon applies NewtonSchulz-style iterations to transform matrix-valued momentum into an orthonormalized update direction, and was introduced as structure-aware optimizer for hidden-layer matrices with strong empirical performance in neural network training [11]. Subsequent large-scale studies further demonstrate that Muon remains effective in language model pretraining when combined with appropriate weight decay and update scaling, supporting orthonormalization-based optimization as practical paradigm beyond small-scale settings [17]. Beyond empirical scaling, recent theory has also begun to explain why orthonormalizationbased updates can be effective, interpreting Muon through non-Euclidean trust-region optimization, norm-constrained optimization, and spectral preconditioning viewpoints [13, 27, 3, 22]. Taken together, these works establish orthonormalization as promising matrix-aware optimization principle and motivate studying the spectral design of matrix-valued updates more directly. Spectral Shaping Beyond Fixed Muon Updates. Recent work has begun to view Muon through broader spectral lens, rather than treating it solely as fixed orthonormalization rule. For instance, [28] places Muon within family of spectral operators of the form ΣpV and studies how different fixed choices of positive connect Muon-style updates to momentum and Adam-like normalization. Other recent extensions further explore richer but still largely fixed or task-specific forms of spectral shaping. For example, Spectra [9] argues that LLM training exhibits persistent spectral anisotropy with dominant spike subspace and long informative tail, and proposes spike-aware shaping that suppresses dominant directions without amplifying the noise-sensitive tail. PRISM [37] augments first-order spectral descent with low-rank quasi-second-order information to perform anisotropic spectral shaping, while SpecMuon [20] introduces mode-wise spectral guidance for scientific machine learning settings with stiff multi-scale dynamics. More broadly, beyond Muon-specific extensions, matrix-aware optimizers such as Shampoo, SOAP, and PolarGrad also exploit non-diagonal geometry and spectral structure to reshape updates beyond simple coordinate-wise scaling [8, 30, 35, 15]. These works establish spectral structure as an important design axis for matrix-valued optimization. However, they mainly study fixed or task-specific spectral transformations. In contrast, DynMuon asks whether the preferred spectral shaping should evolve across different training stages, and studies dynamic spectral shaping rather than single fixed spectral rule. We primarily compare against Muon because DynMuon directly generalizes the Muon update: Muon corresponds to the fixed exponent = 0, whereas DynMuon dynamically varies within the same spectral-shaping family. This provides controlled comparison that isolates the effect of the spectral schedule while keeping the rest of the optimizer design fixed. Muon is also strong and widely used baseline for LLM training, with recent work showing substantial efficiency gains. Matrix-preconditioned optimizers such as Shampoo, SOAP, and PolarGrad are better viewed as complementary to our study rather than as controlled baselines for isolating the effect of dynamic spectral shaping, since they also change the underlying preconditioner and update rule. Dynamic Scheduling in Optimization. long line of work improves training by dynamically scheduling optimizer hyperparameters across different phases of optimization, reflecting the broader principle that different stages of training often benefit from different update behaviors. Classic examples include learning-rate annealing and restart strategies such as SGDR [18], cyclical and one-cycle policies that jointly vary learning rate and momentum [31, 32], and warmup schedules that stabilize adaptive optimizers such as Adam in the early stage of training [21]. Such schedules remain 19 central in modern large-batch and foundation-model training, where warmup and decay of scalar step sizes are often crucial for stable and efficient optimization [38]. More recent work has also begun to study iteration-dependent scaling more directly, for example, by learning online scaling matrices for gradient methods [6], while recent analyses of optimization under ill-conditioned objectives suggest that the relative importance of dominant and bulk subspaces can shift over the course of training [4]. Unlike prior schedules that mainly modulate scalar hyperparameters, DynMuon dynamically schedules the spectral shape of the update itself. This allows the preferred spectral bias to evolve across training rather than remain fixed for the entire run.

sections.title: H Broader Impact
sections.content: This work proposes general-purpose optimization method for training LLMs. Its main potential benefits are improved training efficiency and performance, which may reduce compute costs and energy usage. As technical contribution, the method does not pose direct societal risks. We believe it promotes LLM training, and future work can further address potential concerns by incorporating responsible use guidelines.
